title: Lesson 4 Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Welcome to lesson four, Luis — the first lesson in module two. We'll cover family members, hobbies, the possessive — how to say 'someone's something' — and a really useful Maltese trick called attached pronouns, where the word for 'my', 'your', 'his' and so on becomes a suffix on the noun.
Notice that the masculine and feminine often differ only by the last vowel — u for male, a for female. The same pattern holds for
and the plural for both — children — is
Plurals in Maltese can be irregular: parents combined are
Don't worry about memorising every plural up front — the exercises let you drill them in pairs.
Hobbies. To say what you like to do, you start with
I like... and add the activity. Reading is
Notice the n at the start of each verb — that's the prefix for 'I'. We'll see more about that pattern in lesson five.
Now the possessive. To say something belongs to someone, Maltese uses the small word
which means 'of'. So 'Matthew's dog' becomes 'the dog of Matthew' —
It's like English when you say 'the dog of Matthew' instead of 'Matthew's dog' — same idea, but in Maltese it's the standard word order.
For pronouns — my, your, his, her, our, their — Maltese has full forms first. My is
But here's the powerful shortcut. Instead of saying 'the house of mine', you can attach the pronoun directly to the noun as a suffix. The house
The same suffixes work on most body-parts and family words — my heart is
It's a little like Italian's mio, tuo, suo, but compressed into a single word. Once it clicks, it makes Maltese feel a lot smoother.
That's lesson four. Pick a section and start drilling. Saħħa.
